parseStrategy: The default page load (no query params/POST needed) at https://apps.dnr.wi.gov/beachhealth/ already renders the FULL statewide results table (~700+ rows, ~5000 lines of HTML) with plain server-rendered <span> labels — no JS execution needed (verified: `curl -sL` raw HTML matches WebFetch-rendered content). operator: Wisconsin DNR Beach Monitoring & Notification Program (Wisconsin Beach Health, BEACH Act statewide database)
parseStrategy: The healthmap.aspx page itself is just an ArcGIS JS API (4.29) map shell — raw HTML/curl shows no status text, only a script block with the underlying feature-layer URL: https://dnrmaps.wi.gov/arcgis2/rest/services/OGW_Beach_Monitoring/BEACH_MONITORING_LOCATIONS/MapServer/1 (found via grep for "MapServer" in the raw page source, line 144). This ArcGIS REST service is a public, unauthenticated, live JSON API — far better than HTML scraping. Query: GET https://dnrmaps.wi.gov/arcgis2/rest/services/OGW_Beach_Monitoring/BEACH_MONITORING_LOCATIONS/MapServer/1/query?where=OGW_BEACH_NAME_TEXT+LIKE+%27%25<name>%25%27&outFields=*&f=json&outSR=4326 (outSR=4326 returns WGS84 lat/lon instead of the native WI Transverse Mercator wkid 3071). Parse JSON path features[].attributes: MAP_STATUS (enum values observed: "Open", "Advisory", "Closed", "Closed For Season", "No Data Available" — from the layer's renderer metadata and live records), STATUS (free-text detail e.g. "Open (e coli criteria not exceeded)"), ISSUED (date-range string, e.g. "02-JUL-26 - "), SAMPLEDATE (epoch ms of last water sample), ECOLIVALUE, WATERTEMP. Verified live on 2026-07-05: Otumba Park Beach (ISSUED 02-JUL-26, MAP_STATUS Open), Sunset Park Beach Sturgeon Bay (20-JUN-26, Open), Selner Park (01-JUL-26, Open), Crescent Beach (02-JUL-26, Open), Neshotah Beach (26-JUN-26, Open), Point Beach State Forest - 3 sub-beaches (01-JUL-26, Open). Suggested mapping to swim.report semantics: Open -> green/clear water quality, Advisory -> yellow, Closed -> red, "Closed For Season"/"No Data Available" -> unknown/gray. Statewide dataset has 441 total beach records (returnCountOnly query confirmed).
matchesStrategy: Query the ArcGIS layer filtered by beach name: where=OGW_BEACH_NAME_TEXT LIKE '%<beach short name>%' (case-sensitive substring match worked for all 6 test names: Otumba, Sunset Park, Selner, Crescent Beach, Point Beach — note some names return multiple sub-beach rows, e.g. "Point Beach" matched 6 distinct picnic-area beaches plus two unrelated "Point Beach" name collisions in other counties, so exact-name or lat/lon proximity disambiguation is needed). Geometry returned in outSR=4326 gives lat/lon directly, e.g. Neshotah Beach at (-87.5555, 44.1497), Otumba Park Beach at (-87.3890, 44.8309) — both within the Wisconsin Great Lakes (Lake Michigan/Lake Superior) shoreline bounding box roughly lat 42.5–46.9, lon -92.9 to -86.8. Recommend matching by (a) fuzzy name LIKE query against the D1 beach name, cross-checked with (b) a lat/lon proximity radius (e.g. within ~0.01 deg) against the beach's stored coordinates to disambiguate same-named beaches in different counties, since this is a statewide layer (441 beaches, inland lakes included, not just Great Lakes).

operator: Ohio Department of Health (BeachGuard)
parseStrategy:
The public page (https://publicapps.odh.ohio.gov/BeachGuardPublic/) is a create-react-app SPA: raw HTML is just
plus a script tag for main..js -- confirmed via curl, no status text present in raw HTML. However, decompiling that JS bundle revealed the app calls a public JSON API at the same origin (window.BeachGuardAdmin.servicesPath = document.querySelector('base').href + '/api', i.e. https://publicapps.odh.ohio.gov/beachguardpublic/api/...). Two endpoints are open with NO auth (verified with curl, 200 JSON): (1) GET /beachguardpublic/api/beacheslist -- full statewide registry, 192 beaches, fields id/beachName/countyName/epaBeachId/latitudeCenter/longitudeCenter/waterbodyName/parkName, but 'advisories' and 'monitorings' are null in this bulk call; (2) GET /beachguardpublic/api/beacheslist/{id} -- per-beach detail, same fields plus populated 'monitorings' (array with isCurrentYear, swimSeasonStartDate/EndDate) and 'advisories' (full history array). Other-looking endpoints (advisories, beaches, beachadvisorieslist, mybeacheslist, beachessummary) all return HTTP 401 -- they are admin-only; avoid them. CURRENT status parse: for a given beachId, GET /beacheslist/{id}, read response.queryResults[0].advisories (array); filter entries where advisories[i].isCurrentAdvisory === true. If none -> beach currently has no active advisory (green-equivalent/open). If one or more true -> active advisory now; that entry's reopenDate field reads the literal string 'Ongoing' (vs a real past timestamp for resolved advisories) and reasonTypeText/typeText/typeSeverityLevel describe the hazard (e.g. typeId 'HAB_WARNING_ADV' = algal bloom/toxin advisory, 'CONTAM_ADV' = bacteria contamination advisory, typeSeverityLevel int). LIVE CONFIRMATION: fetched today (2026-07-05) and found beachId 147 (Lake Front Park, Erie County) currently showing isCurrentAdvisory:true, reopenDate:'Ongoing', startDate '07/01/2026 8:51 AM', reasonTypeText 'High bacteria levels' -- proving the mechanism reflects real live status, not stale/archived data. Our 4 target beaches (South Bass Island State Park id=162, Maumee Bay ERIE id=153, Maumee Bay INLAND id=154, Kelleys Island State Park id=148) all currently show zero isCurrentAdvisory:true entries as of this check, i.e. currently open with no active advisory. No separate 'Stone Beach' entry exists anywhere in the 192-beach registry (grepped full JSON for 'stone'), confirming the prior research pass's assumption that it shares South Bass Island's entry (id 162).
matchesStrategy: Hardcode the confirmed ODH BeachGuard numeric beachIds rather than name-matching (source data has odd formatting quirks, e.g. beachName literally 'Maumee Bay State Park (ERIE))' with a stray extra parenthesis): id 162 = South Bass Island State Park (covers 'Stone Beach' too, lat 41.643074 lon -82.839073, Ottawa County); id 153 = Maumee Bay State Park (ERIE) (Lucas County, Lake Erie shore); id 154 = Maumee Bay State Park (INLAND) (Lucas County, inland lake); id 148 = Kelleys Island State Park (Erie/Ottawa County). As a fallback/discovery mechanism, matches(beach) could instead query GET /beachguardpublic/api/beacheslist once, then match candidates via beachName regex /south bass island|maumee bay state park|kelleys island state park/i AND a western Lake Erie bounding box (lat 41.4-41.8, lon -83.5 to -82.6) to guard against name collisions elsewhere in the state.

operator: Algoma Public Health
parseStrategy: Confirmed via `curl -sL` that the live status IS embedded directly in the raw HTML (no JS rendering/AJAX call needed) — WebFetch's markdown conversion strips <script> tags so it wrongly reported "no data visible"; the raw HTML proves otherwise. Around line 274 there is one long inline <script> block containing a flat sequence of: markers.push({ Lat: "46.448444", Long: "-83.762152", Title: "Rock Lake", Image: "/media/1015/google-marker-green.png", PopupText: "Rock Lake" }); repeated ~34 times for the full beach list, all on one line, no line breaks between entries. Regex: /markers\.push\(\{\s*Lat:\s*"(-?[\d.]+)",\s*Long:\s*"\s*(-?[\d.]+)",\s*Title:\s*"([^"]+)",\s*Image:\s*"\/media\/\d+\/(google-marker-(?:green|yellow|red)|orangelocationmarker_\d+)\.png",\s*PopupText:\s*"([^"]*)"\s*\}\);/g — color comes from the Image filename token: 'google-marker-green'->green, 'google-marker-yellow'->yellow, 'google-marker-red'->red, 'orangelocationmarker'->special 'algae' status (legend defines it separately, not seen active in this fetch but its image asset exists at /media/3684/). Footer copyright reads '2026 Algoma Public Health', and fetch date is 2026-07-05 (mid swim season), so this is live in-season data, not archived/static content. As-fetched snapshot: 34 markers, all green except 'Mark's Bay South' (red), 'Centennial Park' (yellow), 'Hiawatha Park' (yellow).
matchesStrategy: Do NOT match by name regex alone — there is a real name collision risk: the marker list contains 'Old Mill Beach' at 46.184497,-82.954016 (near Blind River, ~55km west) that is a DIFFERENT, unrelated beach from the swim.report cluster node also called 'Old Mill Beach' at 46.4485,-83.7627 (Rock Lake / Thessalon River Delta). The APH marker that actually sits ~40m from the swim.report node is titled 'Rock Lake' (46.448444,-83.762152), not 'Old Mill Beach' — so this pairing can only be established via a lat/lon proximity bounding box (e.g. within ~0.001deg / ~100m of a known beach coordinate), never via title-text matching. Recommended matches(beach): true if any parsed marker's (Lat,Long) is within ~100m of beach.lat/beach.lon, regardless of Title string equality. Of the 5 claimed beachNames for this cluster, only 'Old Mill Beach' (wrong location) and 'Spine Beach' (46.384819,-82.680833, exact-name match) and 'Spruce Beach' (46.391256,-82.662440, close but titled 'Spruce Beach' not 'New Beach (Spruce Beach)') appear anywhere on the page; 'The Old Beach' and 'Peace Park' do not appear in the marker array or anywhere else in the raw HTML (grep -in returned zero hits) — those two names are unverified/likely wrong for this source.

operator: Swim Drink Fish (nonprofit) - Swim Guide, mirroring IDEM BeachGuard data
parseStrategy: Raw `curl -sL` HTML (no JS execution) on individual beach pages already contains the full Nuxt SSR payload with current status as literal (non-minified) JSON-like fields — no API call or headless browser needed. Confirmed on 6 target pages (https://www.theswimguide.org/beach/1300, 1303, 1305, 1306, 1311, 1312): each embeds `window.__NUXT__=(function(a,b,...){return {...currentStatus:{...,resultDate:{day:2,month:r,year:s,...},postedDate:{day:3,month:r,year:s,time:"HH:MM"},result:N,waterQuality:{description:"Open|Caution|Closed|No Data Available",type:"CURRENT|HISTORICAL",text:"..."}}...}}(args...))`. The `description` and `type` string values are literal (not minified variable refs) so a simple regex works directly: /waterQuality:\{description:"([^"]+)",type:"(CURRENT|HISTORICAL)"/ — first match in the page is the subject beach's own live record. Map description->color: Open->green, Caution->yellow, Closed->red, 'No Data Available'->unknown/gray. The `type` field (CURRENT vs HISTORICAL) doubles as a built-in staleness flag. Verified postedDate for all 6 beaches = day 3 of the current month/year (month/year values are minified vars resolvable from the trailing IIFE arg list, e.g. beach 1303 args included p=7,q=2026 confirming July 2026), i.e. posted 2 days before the fetch date (2026-07-05/06) — a normal water-quality testing lag, not stale/cached demo data. Statuses differ per beach (Open/Closed/Caution), proving this isn't a static placeholder. No separate JSON/XML API endpoint was found in the HTML (no fetch/axios baseURL, no /api/ or graphql string); the /beaches/indiana list page paginates client-side (query params ?page=2 etc. had no effect, so 'load more' likely uses an internal POST not visible in static HTML) but that doesn't matter since the per-beach pages are the actual scrape target and are fully static-HTML-parseable.
matchesStrategy: Since Swim Guide URLs are opaque numeric IDs (/beach/1300 etc.), not name slugs, matches(beach) requires a small hardcoded id map for the known Indiana Lake Michigan beaches: {1300:'Buffington Harbor Beach', 1301:'Hammond Marina East Beach', 1303:'Jeorse Park Beach I' (also a 'II' entry), 1305:'Lake Street Beach', 1306:'Marquette Park Beach', 1311:'Whihala Beach East', 1312:'Whihala Beach West', ...}. Bound it with a lat/lon box to avoid mismatches: Indiana's Lake Michigan shoreline (Lake/Porter/LaPorte counties) is roughly lat 41.60-41.76, lon -87.50 to -87.15 (confirmed sample: Jeorse Park at locLat:41.64936, locLong:-87.43324). Combine: beach.state==='IN' && lat/lon in that box && name matches one of the mapped literal names (case-insensitive substring, e.g. /buffington|hammond|jeorse|lake street|marquette park|whihala/i).

parseStrategy:

Recommended parse: fetch the endpoint, group by "parent", per beach take the record(s) with type/title containing "Water Quality" for the swim/bacteria status and separately "Surf Conditions"/"Weather" for the flag-pole hazard status; pick the max("date") record per category to avoid stale entries (min observed date in the payload was 2025-08-29, so do not blindly take array[0] as the site's own buggy JS does). Map flag string via /^Red/i -> red (note "Red Afterhours" is a closed-for-hours state, distinguishable by the "Afterhours" substring, not a hazard-red — treat separately if desired), /^Yellow/i -> yellow, /^Green/i -> green, else -> unknown/red-ban.

parseStrategy: Do NOT scrape the category HTML page directly — use the WordPress REST API instead, which is a real, no-JS-needed JSON endpoint discoverable from the page's <link rel="https://api.w.org/"> and confirmed working: GET https://muskegon-mi.gov/wp-json/wp/v2/posts?categories=8&per_page=20&orderby=date&order=desc (category id 8 = slug "muskegon-news", confirmed via GET .../wp-json/wp/v2/categories/8). There is no dedicated per-beach "status" field — you must text-match each post's title+content against: (a) a beach-name regex /Beachwood|Harbou?r Towne|Pere Marquette/i and (b) an advisory/closure keyword regex /advisory|e\.?\s*coli|closure|closed|unsafe (for|to) swim/i. Coli Advisory for Harbor Towne Park Beach" — the "[LIFTED]" prefix (or content phrase "has been lifted"/"is open again for swimming") signals resolved/clear; a title like "High E. Coli Advisory Issued for X Beach" without "[LIFTED]" and content "Muskegon County has issued a high E. coli advisory" signals an active advisory/closure. (B) Embedded JSON (window.__NUXT__ SSR payload) — richer but fragile: it's a minified Nuxt dedup-string IIFE `(function(a,b,c,...){return {...}})(actualValues...)`. The `return` object contains `beach.currentStatus:{resultDate:{day,month,year},postedDate:{day,month,year,time},waterQuality:{description,type,text}}` and `beach.locLat`/`locLong`, but month/year/lat/lon fields are single-letter variable refs that must be resolved against the trailing call-argument list (e.g. saw locLat=41.643572, locLong=-82.837961, year=2026, month=6 resolved this way). Usable but brittle if Nuxt's minifier output changes shape — recommend (A) as primary, (B) only as a fallback cross-check. No public unauthenticated JSON/REST API was found: probed https://api.theswimguide.org (no DNS), https://www.theswimguide.org/api/beach/709 (401 Unauthorized), and a guessed irc.swimguide.org endpoint (no DNS) — none usable. Page is a per-beach static-ID route (/beach/709); no lat/lon or name search API is exposed.
matchesStrategy: This is a single per-beach page keyed by an opaque numeric Swim Guide ID (709), not derivable from beach name or coordinates alone — there is no public search-by-location API. A `matches(beach)` predicate would need: name regex /south bass island|stone beach/i AND/OR a lat/lon bounding box around Put-in-Bay / South Bass Island, Lake Erie, OH (~41.635–41.655 N, -82.85–-82.82 W, confirmed locLat=41.643572/locLong=-82.837961 from the embedded payload), PLUS a hardcoded static map of {beach name/coords → swimguide numeric ID} maintained per covered beach, since the ID itself can't be computed. Note this beach (Put-in-Bay, Lake Erie, Ohio) falls outside swim.report's current Overpass discovery pilot bbox (Michigan/Great Lakes per CLAUDE.md), so it isn't yet in scope unless the pilot area expands to include Ohio's Lake Erie shoreline.

operator: Swim Guide (Swim Drink Fish Canada), sourcing Michigan EGLE water-quality data
parseStrategy:
Fetch https://www.theswimguide.org/beach/1071 with a normal browser UA. The page is a Nuxt.js SSR app — the data is already embedded in the raw HTML inside a script tag as a JS object literal, no headless browser needed: `window.__NUXT__=(function(a,b,c,...){return {...}})(null,0,"",true,1,1071,...)`. Within that literal, the beach's live status sits in a stable, fixed key shape regardless of the minified variable names around it:
  currentStatus:{id:f,historicalRating:o,manualRating:o,resultDate:a,postedDate:a,result:3,waterQuality:{description:"No Data Available",type:"CURRENT",text:"No data available"}}
Regex: /waterQuality:\{description:"([^"]*)",type:"CURRENT",text:"([^"]*)"\}/ captures the human-readable status (description + text). Also capture resultDate/postedDate tokens (ISO strings when non-null, else the shared "null" variable) directly preceding waterQuality in the same object.
MANDATORY guard before trusting the match: verify the payload's own beach id (either the leading `data:[{id:"1071"` token or `routePath:"\/beach\/1071"` near the end of the same script block) equals the beach id you requested. One of four back-to-back fetches of this exact URL during verification returned a full, well-formed SSR payload for a completely different beach (id 1212, "Harbor Beach City Park", ~250mi away) under the /beach/1071 URL — an apparent CDN/SSR cache anomaly. Any scraper must diff the embedded id against the requested id and return null on mismatch (this is also required by the repo's "parse defensively / degrade to null" scraper contract).
No usable API: the beach object's own `feedUrl` field points to "http://translate.theswimguide.org/michigan/json" (the presumed upstream EGLE-to-SwimGuide translation feed) — both http and https variants return HTTP 500 with an empty body, so that shortcut is dead; HTML/embedded-JS scraping is the only path.
matchesStrategy: Name regex: /Lighthouse\s*Park|Sylvan\s*Beach(\s*Kids\s*Beach)?/i, AND lat/lon proximity to the single externally-tracked point at 43.37341, -86.4257 (White Lake channel, Whitehall, MI) — e.g. bounding box lat 43.365–43.380, lon -86.435 to -86.415 (~0.5–1 mi radius) to catch both of our separately-mapped points (Lighthouse Park, Sylvan Beach Kids Beach) that this one Swim Guide page covers jointly.

operator: Door County Public Health (in partnership with UW-Oshkosh Environmental Research & Innovation Center)
parseStrategy: The claimed URL (https://wibeaches.us) is NOT itself a scrapable page — curl -sIL and WebFetch both confirm it is a vanity domain that 301-redirects (Location header) to the generic https://dnr.wisconsin.gov/topic/Beaches informational hub, which contains no per-beach status data at all, only marketing copy and a list of promo-card links. So the original research finding is wrong as stated. However, one of those linked promo cards (raw HTML anchor at line 486 of the fetched page: <a href="https://apps.dnr.wi.gov/beachhealth/healthmap.aspx" ... aria-label="Current Beach Status">) leads to the real, live, statewide WI DNR Beach Health map. That map's raw HTML (fetched via curl) embeds an Esri JS API call at line 144: url: "https://dnrmaps.wi.gov/arcgis2/rest/services/OGW_Beach_Monitoring/BEACH_MONITORING_LOCATIONS/MapServer/1" — a directly queryable ArcGIS Feature Service, no JS rendering required. Query pattern verified working: GET https://dnrmaps.wi.gov/arcgis2/rest/services/OGW_Beach_Monitoring/BEACH_MONITORING_LOCATIONS/MapServer/1/query?where=OGW_BEACH_NAME_TEXT+LIKE+%27%25Otumba%25%27&outFields=*&f=json returns JSON features with attributes.OGW_BEACH_NAME_TEXT, attributes.STATUS (human string e.g. "Open (e coli criteria not exceeded)"), attributes.BEACH_ACTION, attributes.MAP_STATUS (enum: Open / Advisory / Closed / Closed For Season / No Data Available), attributes.ISSUED (date range string), attributes.SAMPLEDATE (epoch ms), attributes.ECOLIVALUE, attributes.WATERTEMP, and attributes.DNR_SWIMS_ID (stable per-beach ID). Live-verified today (2026-07-05): "Otumba Park Beach" DNR_SWIMS_ID 10012374, MAP_STATUS "Open", ISSUED "02-JUL-26 -" (3 days old); "Sunset Park Beach Sturgeon Bay" DNR_SWIMS_ID 10014571, MAP_STATUS "Open", ISSUED "20-JUN-26 -". Both are genuinely current, in-season readings, not archived/static content. Recommended integration: query this ArcGIS FeatureServer layer 1 by DNR_SWIMS_ID (preferred, stable) or OGW_BEACH_NAME_TEXT substring, map MAP_STATUS to color (Open→green, Advisory→yellow/red, Closed→red, "Closed For Season"/"No Data Available"→unknown/gray), and cite source as "Wisconsin DNR Beach Health Program" — NOT wibeaches.us or Door County Public Health as originally claimed.
matchesStrategy: Do not match on the literal wibeaches.us domain claim. For the real source, match Door County Lake Michigan beaches by exact DNR_SWIMS_ID whitelist (10012374 = Otumba Park Beach, 10014571 = Sunset Park Beach Sturgeon Bay) fetched once and cached, or by name regex /^(Otumba Park Beach|Sunset Park Beach.*Sturgeon Bay)$/i against OGW_BEACH_NAME_TEXT. If a lat/lon box is preferred (the FeatureServer's native geometry is in WKID 3071, Wisconsin Transverse Mercator, not lat/lon — would need reprojection), Door County Lake Michigan/Green Bay beaches fall roughly within lat 44.6-45.3 N, lon -87.6 to -86.8 W; note this box would also catch unrelated beaches elsewhere in the same layer (it's a statewide dataset), so the SWIMS_ID whitelist is the more reliable matcher.
